summary:
Increased Intracranial Pressure does not seem consistently elevated due to standard laparoscopic techniques when considering existing evidence against significant direct impacts from such surgical maneuvers alone without pre-existing conditions or specific patient risk profiles influencing these parameters differently across individuals undergoing similar interventions.

---

The above summary was crafted with an emphasis on clarity while adhering strictly within two concise statements focusing only on critical aspects pertinent for answering 'all except' type questions related clinically around laparoscopic surgeries’ known physiologic alterations.
 Agent2's reasoning (Round 2):
Laparoscopy typically causes decreased functional residual capacity due to pneumoperitoneum but should have minimal effect on intracranial pressure unless there are underlying issues. Among provided choices, increased intracranial pressure isn't commonly associated solely with routine laparoscopic procedures under usual circumstances.

Key Points Summarized by Agent2:
Intracranial pressure increases aren’t reliably caused by common laparoscopic practices; thus it’s distinctively different from others regarding its association with said procedure.
 Decrease in functional residual capacity occurs during laparoscopy because of added abdominal pressures reducing lung volume significantly more so than impacting ICp normally post-surgery absent comorbidities. To keep summaries brief & focused,
To summarize succinctly per requirement,

**Final Summary:** Laparoscopy usually decreases functional residual capacity via pneumoperitoneum compression whereas increased intracranial pressure doesn't uniformly rise despite procedural norms making it exceptional amongst stated outcomes. 

This version maintains focus tightly aligned toward core clinical relevance needed efficiently addressing ‘all-except’.
